VideoTitle: Oracle owns Javascript and is sending notices
CommentText: I attribute this to the idiotic patent system in the US. They trademark everything imaginable for incredibly long periods. Originally, copyright was set for 14 years, which makes sense for inventors to capitalize and cover costs. Even 20 years seems fine. Now, Disney's copyright goes up to 120 years, and it happens in other areas too, such as Javascript being trademarked even though it has nothing to do with Oracle. There is not an official gain as Javascript is officially EcmaScript, but they can still create problems. I think the US government is at fault for this terrible patent system.
CommentTextWithContext: Video Title: Oracle owns Javascript and is sending notices. Comment: I attribute this to the idiotic patent system in the US. Now, Disney's copyright goes up to 120 years, and it happens in other areas too, such as Javascript being trademarked even though it has nothing to do with Oracle. VideoTitle: 5 Design Patterns Every Engineer Should Know
CommentText: "Singleton pattern" is not a valid pattern for C++.  It's an anti-pattern.  In C++, you have no control over its life cycle.  The static class scoped variable is not explicitly controlled by the developer.  In older versions of C++, the constructor is not threadsafe and in the embedded space, this is still the case.  Thus, less portable code....

VideoTitle: Public Video and Data Feeds of Highway License Plate Readers
CommentText: I wonder what part of the system "guesses" to fill in missing license plate data?.?.  About 15 years ago I bought a car in FL and drove it home to TN.  I went by the local FL dmv to buy a temp tag as I had a long drive home.  Well out of the blue last year I started getting toll citations from FL...wtf...I hadn't been to FL in a decade.    You couldn't make out the plate and when I called the toll administrators in FL they said the system basically guesses to fill in unreadable plates...again...wtf!!!  This shifts the burden to prove innocence onto the unlucky person the system guesses was the violator.

VideoTitle: How not to take things personally? | Frederik Imbo | TEDxMechelen
CommentText:
Some notes:
-Reasons we talk things personally is because of ego and the desire to be right. 
-Solutions: 
1) It's not about me. Look at WE. What's their intention. understand where they are coming from . For example, they want to be right, maybe they have a call they have been waiting for. 

2)- deal with your negative beliefs. We take things personally because we know there is some truth in it. Big chance this is rooted in your childhood. So give yourself some empathy. 
3) By telling people how you feel without blaming others might increase the chance of people taking your feelings into account.

Remember whatever others do, you will always keep your value.

VideoTitle: Failing at Normal: An ADHD Success Story | Jessica McCabe | TEDxBratislava
CommentText: I couldn't hold back the tears. My younger years were like Jessica's—full of academic success and recognition—but as I got older and the executive functioning demands increased, I just couldn't keep up. I've always felt like I have to work so much harder than others to simply organize my life without losing my sanity. I just got finished listening to my husband rant about how inattentive I am, how I procrastinate all the time, how forgetful I am, how I choose to focus on some things and not others...I try to explain but he just doesn't get it. I don't TRY to be distracted or disorganized—in fact, I dedicate a huge amount of brain power to fighting ADHD symptoms, but sometimes I get so overwhelmed that I just CRASH. I guess I appear somewhat "put together" to those who don't know me well (they only see the results of projects that I've hyperfocused on), which only causes me anxiety because I fail to carry that same level of excellence into every aspect of life. I feel so misunderstood.

VideoTitle: Top Programming Languages in 2020
CommentText: What's the basis for 'Top 3'? Terms like 'top,' 'best,' 'worst,' and 'bad' aren't really applicable in programming. Some languages are better suited for specific tasks, but that doesn't make others inferior. Rather than focusing excessively on finding the 'best' language (which doesn't exist), concentrate on enhancing your programming skills: 1. SOLID principles, 2. Design Patterns, 3. Data structures, 4. Algorithms, and 5. Testing. All programming languages are valuable; it's a matter of preference. I've used PHP since 2004 for web applications, along with JavaScript. I utilize Python for small web APIs and computer vision with TensorFlow and OpenCV. I've also worked with Go, Elixir, Ruby, Objective-C, Swift, C, and R, among other technologies. Recently, out of 56 web developer applicants, only one wrote unit tests. I can't consider you a developer if you don't test or write code that's hard to read and adds complexity.

Rather than focusing excessively on finding the 'best' language (which doesn't exist), concentrate on enhancing your programming skills: 1. SOLID principles, 2. Design Patterns, 3. Data structures, 4. Algorithms, and 5. Testing. All programming languages are valuable; it's a matter of preference. I've used PHP since 2004 for web applications, along with JavaScript. I utilize Python for small web APIs and computer vision with TensorFlow and OpenCV. I've also worked with Go, Elixir, Ruby, Objective-C, Swift, C, and R, among other technologies. Recently, out of 56 web developer applicants, only one wrote unit tests. I can't consider you a developer if you don't test or write code that's hard to read and adds complexity.

VideoTitle: new year's resolutions, christmas gifts, + room makeover!
CommentText: HAPPY NEW YEAR! I just officially incorporated my business this morning and I am SO EXCITED for the year ahead! I hope anyone reading this had a wonderful holiday season and a happy new year! And remember, no matter where you are in life, it CAN get better! Staring my dumpling business has been a decades long dream that I all but gave up on after the car accident in 2013. But, after a little over a decade of extreme lows that I thought each one would surely kill me- literally and metaphorically- I am still here and reframing my perspective. What I once mourned as my 30’s lost, I am now seeing as my 40’s gained. Vera Wang didn’t sew her first gown until she was 40. Anthony Bourdain found his passion and success at 40. With my 41st just around the corner, I decided to take the leap. There’s so much I don’t know and there are going to be growing pains, but if I can survive what I already have, I know this is achievable. And I KNOW each and every one of us can achieve our dreams, too. Please never ever give up! ❤

VideoTitle: Pygame Tutorial - Creating Space Invaders
CommentText: If anyone is wondering why the Y coordinate increases going down instead of up, like it does in mathematics, I believe the reason is because that is how screen rasterization on the old CRTs were done.  It would draw from the top-left corner and go to the right first, then back and down a line to draw the next row, etc.  Why did that do it that way?   Probably because that was the way people read books here in the west, left to right and top to bottom in the same way.  So maybe that will help you remember how the coordinate systems work in graphics programming.
CommentTextWithContext: Video Title: Pygame Tutorial - Creating Space Invaders. CommentText:
I'm comparing AP with Secretariat here. Amazing horses, off- the-map athletes! Super stars. Stud fees of course also off the map. None of Secretariat's get ever made the news. HE was the one off! American Pharoah is just like Secretariat, a one off. His get will never match himself. 

These super stars can't be engendered, or reproduced, through their get. They, themselves are one-offs. The stars crossed, the DNA met, and these wonderful animals came to us. There's no re-producing these super stars. Never, in the history of horse racing, has their ever been an immediate reproduction of a super star. It takes generations, and a lot of outside bloodlines to make the wonder mix. 

Like the Royal Family, or the Mennonites, you have to slip something different in there once in a while to perk up the DNA.

VideoTitle: Introduction to React Js + Installation | Complete React Course in Hindi #1
CommentText:
REVIEW: I am currently at video no 55 and  as per my experience this is a must course for all students who have finished their basics in html,css and js and now wan to enter in react from the very basics. The best thing about this course is the gradual increase in difficulty. The first app(Textutils) was solely a front end app introducing us to the basics of reacts. Second one we got one step further and started interacting with an API and using our REACT frontend and learnt some imp react concepts along the way like hooks, lifecylce methods etc. The third app (at which I currently am) is even one step further and we are making our own api  as well as frontend using the full MERN stack. Not only this gives a wonderful encouragement and start to MERN stack but the amount of learnings in every video is huge. I recommend taking notes of imp points.

Finally Thanks harry bhai for this course.👍
CommentTextWithContext:

The best thing about this course is the gradual increase in difficulty. The first app(Textutils) was solely a front end app introducing us to the basics of reacts. Second one we got one step further and started interacting with an API and using our REACT frontend and learnt some imp react concepts along the way like hooks, lifecylce methods etc. The third app (at which I currently am) is even one step further and we are making our own api  as well as frontend using the full MERN stack. VideoTitle: REVIEW: Taylor Swift's Feminist Documentary Disaster! | Louder with Crowder
CommentText:
This is an emotional based reaction I've ever seen. I'm so dissappointed how this reaction only highlight what the view doesn't like. I read the comments and I think that most of the views or the commenters haven't watch the documentary film with open hearts. When I watch half of this reaction video, I almost drown with the opinions especially the bias opinion. As a non native speaker (of english), I think Taylor Swift is a smart person and very kind. Maybe, as non asian people, you think that 'pregnancy accusion' or whatever you called it not bad to anyone and should be normal but the reality is, that is not good. Just imagine your daughter being accused have pregnant. Just imagine how trauma and phobia Taylor Swift was. I know she writes about her exes and I still have no idea how hurt she was that lead her to write. 

I have no idea. But, as a man, a person, we must except that there is someone like Taylor Swift. She has been through hardship and no one would manage to get over and move on. Yes, she is rich but still, she has her reputation. She's trying to show that she's just human being, a person even though she is a millionaire. We need to stop being toxic with saying 'all human, any gender through hardships too. Not just her'. For people say that, she look like a student instead of 30 years old woman, you maybe right but Taylor Swift just being herself no matter what her old is. For people say that she should married for any reason; marriage is not the key to stop thinking the way she think. Even she married, there will be something bad you still see from her. If you hate her, just being honest and tell the reason. For goodness sake, watch her documentary carefully and accept the truth that she is not playing 'the victim'. She just share her point of view. And, only her fans, family and friends know what she has been through. There something I don't agree about her stand and whatsoever, but, I still like Taylor Swift because she is clever, diligent and successful. 

No hate. Just my point of view.

VideoTitle: #31 Python Tutorial for Beginners | Working with Matrix in Python
CommentText:
from numpy import *

m1 = array([
            [1,2],
            [3,4]
            ])

m2 = array([
            [2,1],
            [4,3]
            ])
m3=array([[0,0],
         [0,0]])
for i in range(2):
    for j in range(2):
        for k in range(2):
            m3[i,j] = m1[i,k] * m2[k,j] + m3[i,j]

print(m1)
print(m2)
print(m3)

VideoTitle: Ukraine War: Russia end 'operational pause'
CommentText: 1000 tons of ammo + propulsive charge + boxes...that's a lot. Ask the Germans who did this kind of firepower exercise in 1916 on the opening of Verdun. It's not sustainable on the long term. And Russians have reached peaks in Severdonetsk of 40.000 shells per day. Russia is believed to have a stock of 15M shells. So they're not going to run out soon at this firerate. And they're probably producing a LOT of these in their factories too. The problem are the logistics that are a nightmare. With HIMARS killing their depots & stockpiles, the tide will turn. Russia has no more modern tanks in numbers nor the manpower. It's going downhill. All they have is artillery and missiles. Key Takeaways

1. push() method in array simply add the array as an new single entity in the existing array.
2. concat() method help us achieving a desisered output, like merging the two array, but the problems with concat are, It complicated to concat more then one array simultaneously and also It takes extra space and do the merging operation.
3. Despite push and concat we should use spread operator.
4. Intresting methods, isArray(), from() and of()

VideoTitle: Will Devin AI Replace Coders?
CommentText:
Everyone conveniently leaves out the future of this technology though. 

First year computer science students need to know what is going to happen because college is just so expensive. 

The motive for companies is there to reduce the number of developers. They are extremely expensive. There will always be a need for computing professionals, but less computing professionals will be needed for projects. 

The only true light in the dark is if this technology allows more companies to grow and increase their output. More output = more engineers, even if there was less of a need for them.

Also there is also the fact that you have to specialize. You can't just be a generic app developer anymore, you need to specialize in the type of app like games or web apps, etc... 

I hope I'm making sense. Correct me if I'm wrong of course.

VideoTitle: How to learn any language in six months | Chris Lonsdale | TEDxLingnanUniversity
CommentText:
Hey, my native language is Spanish, when I was 8 years I went to the US 3 months, I went to a school I was more a listener.
I didn't talk with the other kids because, I only knew how to say hello, bye, some colors and numbers, I watched videos in English, I heard people talking in english everyday, without noticing it, I was able to understand, I remember telling my mom "Mom! I had a conversation with a girl, I asked her what was her favorite color" Yes, it was very very simple, but for me and my mom wasn't.

I learned English literally by myself, no one translated for me.

The last year I improved a lot, good luck to everyone! 
If you want to learn a language, you have to be motivated, you have to take it seriously, try listening songs in that language, watch videos in that language, even if you don't understand, that will help you.

Right now I'm learning Japanese, Korean and French.

I will comeback in 6 months!

Hi! 
I forgot to come, sorry, I have been learning a lot, I’m also in school since October so I don’t have too much time to progress, it has been a little bit difficult, specially grammar, but so far, I progressed a lot, if we talk about Korean, I can understand a lot now.

Don’t give up! You can do it! :)

CommentText: I'm on the same page as you guys regarding the whole "you can't bring the dog...blah blah...ADA...blah blah..." thing. That's beside the point, and we should all get that! The thing is, she didn't even think twice about it, just smiled and strolled off. I'll have to try that next time I get pulled over. Officer: "License and registration, please." Me: "Oops, forgot my license at home!" Big grin. Then I'll just drive off, and everything should be fine, right?!?!! Just like in this scenario, if it were real, those officers could have arrested her for evading the police. What should have happened is they should have asked/told her to stay off the beach until she has either the proper license or a vest/identifier on the dog. Those vests aren't just for the human, and they're definitely not just cute outfits for the animals! They're meant for the public's awareness! They let people know that this isn't just a "cute puppy" or a "pretty pot belly piggy." It's a piece of medical equipment, so hands off!

CommentText:
Billionaires, by the very nature of their existence, embody a profound moral failing: they hoard resources in a world of scarcity, prioritizing their own limitless accumulation over the basic needs of millions. This concentration of wealth is not neutral—it corrodes democracy and undermines civilization itself. 

In a just society, power stems from collective effort and shared prosperity, not the whims of the few who exploit economic systems for personal gain. To amass billions is to claim dominion over the futures of others. It is an affront human dignity and the moral fabric that binds us as equals.

VideoTitle: Validate Binary Search Tree - Depth First Search - Leetcode 98
CommentText:
A slightly better time solution. As soon as we reach the k value we break from the recursive loop:

res=[]
        def dfs(node):
            if not node:
                return
            dfs(node.left)
            res.append(node.val)
            if len(res)==k:
                return 
            dfs(node.right)
        dfs(root)
        return res[k-1]
CommentTextWithContext:
Video Title: Validate Binary Search Tree - Depth First Search - Leetcode 98. Comment: A slightly better time solution. As soon as we reach the k value we break from the recursive loop:

res=[]
        def dfs(node):
            if not node:
                return
            dfs(node.left)
            res.append(node.val)
            if len(res)==k:
                return 
            dfs(node.right)
        dfs(root)
        return res[k-1]

Now they've learned everything about what makes a car door sound robust and the interior feel premium—all the elements that are captivating. Consider Denza, the partnership between BYD and Mercedes: Mercedes is now out of the picture, and Denza, along with vehicles like Huawei's Stelato, are squeezing the German manufacturers out of the market, similar to how they pushed the German pharmaceutical industry out of the market and many other sectors.

VideoTitle: Coding Challenge #85: The Game of Life
CommentText: How to Truly Live Life: Why are children full of energy and excitement when they wake up, while adults reach for their phones to snooze the alarm? Why are young people eager to "conquer the world," while older people are more careful? Can we keep that youthful spirit forever? After all, if life isn't exciting, what's the point? To keep enjoying, we need to see life as a lifelong game. This game isn't about what we get, but what we discover! Connecting with others opens up new worlds - their worlds! How can we do this? By focusing on others instead of ourselves. It sounds easy, but you'll see how self-centered we are. Even generous people focus on their own good deeds and miss how much they do for themselves. Giving is great, but the focus matters. If we focused on the receiver, we'd see hidden things. To succeed, you need partners. You can't play the giving game alone. A group of up to ten people can play together. To understand each other, we need to think positively about each other. Otherwise, we won't want to connect. Doing good things for each other helps us think positively. These can be simple things, but they make us feel good. Positive thoughts bring us closer, opening our minds to see the world through their eyes. Like any game, practice makes perfect, making it harder but more rewarding. But unlike other games, this changes our perspective on life, making us wiser and more skilled. This game reveals life's secrets because seeing things from all angles hides nothing.

VideoTitle: I Bought And Rebuilt A Wrecked Exotic Car You’ve Never Heard Of (1000 Miles From Home)
CommentText: I love the purple.  We have the odd couple of guys in my town that change the Opel badges of their vehicles to Vauxhall badges.  Yea we all know its the same car but that's just silly.  That said, I think you would want to keep the Holden badges on this SS.  If you want to change to Chevy then you also need to put the Mrs on the right hand side (Swop the peddles and wheel pointer to the other side).  Kudos  for giving the vehicle away mate.  In my country (South Africa) vehicles are incredibly expensive due to extremely high taxes and the cost of living here is getting ridiculous with less than 7% of the population paying tax whilst almost 40% of the (working age) population on some form of government pension.  Its a breath of fresh air watching what you guys get up to on that side of the big pond.  Regards and keep up the lovely episodes.
